read.dek: The lecture asks where secret societies come from and answers by rebuilding Western religion as a sequence of world models: womb, war, empire, false God, inner light, and poetry as an encoded map back to the Monad.
read.thesis: Evil is born when one true God turns reality into obedience. In Jiang's reconstruction, mother-goddess religion begins from mind, fertility, and cosmic harmony; war creates property and pantheons; empire creates monotheism, truth, evil, and the individual. Secret societies preserve the suppressed counter-story: this world is a prison made by the Demiurge, the true God is inside, and transgression becomes the dangerous route by which the soul discovers knowledge, wisdom, and return.

opening.heading: Core Reading
opening.text: The lecture's pressure comes from a reversal of the usual religious story. Secret societies do not begin as villains in a dark room. They begin, in this account, as the underground survival of an older religious imagination. Agriculture makes the womb divine. War turns women into property and gods into pantheons. Empire compresses the world into one true God. Once there is one true God, there can be one truth, one evil, and one individual soul standing outside family or community. That is the birth of evil as a category: not disadvantage, not bad luck, not injustice against cosmic law, but disobedience against the one God.

beats.heading: War Invents Property
beats.summary: Population pressure creates discarded young men, raiding, wives as reward, property, hierarchy, marriage, and gods arranged as a pantheon.

paragraphs.text: The switch to polytheism begins with war. Populations rise, resources tighten, and young men without families become disposable. They need wives to survive, so they raid other societies. Jiang's image is deliberately brutal: they become a wolf pack. The reward for fighting is a wife who belongs to the warrior; property, hierarchy, and marriage appear together.

beats.heading: Empire Makes One Truth
beats.summary: Rome makes monotheism possible by imposing reality; one true God produces truth, evil, and the individual, the three forces Jiang says underpin modernity.

paragraphs.text: Monotheism arrives with empire because empire can impose one reality. A war society still needs people; Rome can destroy whole societies and make its God the God of everyone. That is the political condition for one true God. Once there is one true God, the world becomes simpler and more violent at the level of concept: truth is moving toward God, evil is moving away, and the individual is the person who can step outside community in the name of faith.

beats.heading: The False God

beats.heading: Poetry As Initiation
beats.summary: Paradise Lost is treated as encoded secret knowledge: Satan's heroic escape speech and his argument to Eve become initiation into risk, transgression, and knowledge.

paragraphs.text: If ordinary people cannot bear the truth directly, poetry becomes the hiding place. Paradise Lost is not just a national epic. It is a text that becomes part of the elite soul, a poem whose secrets only appear to readers who already know how to look. Milton's blindness makes him prophet-like in this telling: the poem is spoken out of darkness as revelation.

Initiation means choosing alone to fight for humanity while knowing the world may hunt you for speaking truth.

beats.heading: Satan Tells The Truth
beats.summary: Genesis is read against the official classroom interpretation: the serpent's promise comes true, banishment prevents divinization, and Eve knows through the inner light.

paragraphs.text: The sharpest reversal comes when Jiang puts university Milton against Genesis. The classroom version says Satan lies and Eve is stupid. The Bible, he argues, says the opposite: the serpent says they will not die and their eyes will open; they eat, do not die, and gain knowledge. The banishment is not the punishment. Pain in childbirth and hard labor are punishment. Banishment is containment.

paragraphs.text: The Nephilim return at the end as the bridge to mythology. The sons of God are the old gods: Zeus, Ares, Apollo. Their children with mortal women are the old heroes: Hercules, Achilles, Theseus. The Bible becomes a tremendous act of propaganda, rewriting polytheistic heroes as dangerous offspring under one supreme God and denying the older intuition of humanity.

beats.heading: The Light Inside
beats.summary: Two final student questions let Jiang state the practical secret: do not pray outward; look inward, make mistakes, and let the light redeem you.

paragraphs.text: The final question about prayer gives the episode its ethical close. According to these secret societies, praying to an outside force means praying to the false God. The true God is inside. The answer is not surrender but self-trust: look inside, believe enough to find the truth, and recognize that the conflict between devil and God, good and evil, happens through the human soul.

paragraphs.text: This is the most dangerous sentence in the lecture: it is okay to make mistakes, even to do evil, if you listen to the light and redeem yourself. That does not make evil good. It makes error the material of wisdom. The Monad rewards self-belief, and self-belief often means defying authority, breaking rules, and returning through the consequences with more imagination than before.

Jiang gives two explanations: either the angels are wicked because they come from the false God, or beauty itself draws divine beings and humans together. Then the lecture closes by naming the next problem. Secret societies begin as a noble mission; the question is how they become corrupted over time.
